questions.text: What is the biblical source for the mitzvah of hafrashas challah?

options.label: A
options.text: Devarim 18 – the portion of the Kohen.

options.label: B
options.text: Bamidbar 15:17-21 – when the Jewish people enter the land, a portion of dough must be given to the Kohen.

options.label: C
options.text: Vayikra 23 – the mitzvos of the Moadim.

options.label: D
options.text: Shemos 25 – the terumah for the Mishkan.

sourceRef.section: A. Introduction to the Mitzvah of Challah
sourceRef.snippet: The Torah in Bamidbar 15:17-21 describes that upon entering the land, a portion of dough (terumah) must be given to the Kohen.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): The Torah refers to hafrashas challah as "terumah."

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: A. Introduction to the Mitzvah of Challah
sourceRef.snippet: The Torah uses the word 'terumah' to refer to hafrashas challah, as stated in Bamidbar 15:20-21.

questions.text: According to Rashi and the Sifri, when did the mitzvah of hafrashas challah go into effect?

options.label: A
options.text: Only after the land of Israel was fully conquered and settled (14 years after crossing the Jordan).

options.label: B
options.text: Only after the Beis HaMikdash was built.

options.label: C
options.text: As soon as the Jewish people entered the land, even before it was conquered and settled.

options.label: D
options.text: Only after the majority of Jews lived in Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: A. Introduction to the Mitzvah of Challah
sourceRef.snippet: Rashi quotes the Sifri: regarding other mitzvos the Torah uses 'ki savo'u' (when you come), but here it says 'b'vo'achem' – the mitzvah went into effect as soon as they entered the land, even before conquest and settlement.

questions.text: According to the Rambam, what is the status of the mitzvah of hafrashas challah today?

options.label: A
options.text: Biblical in Eretz Yisrael, does not apply in chutz la'aretz.

options.label: B
options.text: It is only a minhag, not an obligation.

options.label: C
options.text: Biblical everywhere.

options.label: D
options.text: Rabbinic – both in Eretz Yisrael and in chutz la'aretz.

sourceRef.section: A. Introduction > Gemara Kesubos 25a
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Huna reads 'b'vo'achem' as requiring all Jews to be in the land. Since not all entered during Bayis Sheini, challah was only rabbinic. Today, with even fewer Jews in EY, it remains rabbinic according to the Rambam.

questions.text: Why is hafrashas challah observed in chutz la'aretz?

options.label: A
options.text: Because it is a biblical commandment that applies everywhere.

options.label: B
options.text: So that the mitzvah will not be forgotten.

options.label: C
options.text: Because the kedusha of Eretz Yisrael extends to the entire world.

options.label: D
options.text: It is only observed as a stringency, not an obligation.

sourceRef.section: A. Introduction to the Mitzvah of Challah
sourceRef.snippet: Chazal enacted hafrashas challah in chutz la'aretz in order that the mitzvah won't be forgotten (שלא תשתכח תורת חלה).

questions.text: According to the Chazon Ish, what is needed for the mitzvah of challah to become biblical again?

options.label: A
options.text: The majority of Jews must live in Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: B
options.text: The Beis HaMikdash must be rebuilt.

options.label: C
options.text: The Jewish people may need to enter the land under the direction of a king or navi.

options.label: D
options.text: All 12 tribes must be identified and settled on their ancestral land.

sourceRef.section: B. Status of Hafrashas Challah Today
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish holds that challah may need the Jewish people to enter under the direction of a king or navi for it to become biblical again, unlike terumah which depends on majority presence.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): According to the Chazon Ish, regarding terumah, if the majority of Jews return to Eretz Yisrael, the mitzvah of terumah will become biblical – even without a king or navi.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: B. Status of Hafrashas Challah Today
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish distinguishes: terumah will become biblical when the majority of Jews return to EY, even without a king or navi. Challah, however, may require entry under direction of a king or navi since it took effect upon entry regardless of majority.

questions.text: Rav Chaim Kanievsky suggests that the mitzvah of terumah may remain rabbinic today because:

options.label: A
options.text: The Beis HaMikdash has not been rebuilt.

options.label: B
options.text: The ten lost tribes are still unaccounted for, so the majority of Jews may not actually be in Israel.

options.label: C
options.text: Ezra did not sanctify the land a second time.

options.label: D
options.text: The mitzvah only applies with a Sanhedrin.

sourceRef.section: B. Status of Hafrashas Challah Today
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Chaim Kanievsky suggests that even if there are currently more than six million Jews in EY, the ten lost tribes are still unaccounted for, so the majority of the total Jewish population may not actually be in Israel.

questions.text: What are the three differences between challah of Eretz Yisrael and challah of chutz la'aretz discussed in the source? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: The bitul (nullification) ratios when challah mixes with permitted bread.

options.label: B
options.text: Whether you may eat the bread before being mafrish challah.

options.label: C
options.text: Whether you may be mafrish from bread not physically present before you (shelo min hamukaf).

options.label: D
options.text: Whether the challah needs to be burned or may be eaten.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences Between Challah of EY and Chutz La'aretz
sourceRef.snippet: Three distinctions: 1) Bitul ratios – CL is lenient (S"A: even 50-50; Rama: 100:1). 2) CL allows eating before mafrish. 3) CL permits hafrasha shelo min hamukaf (from bread not in front of you).

questions.text: According to the S"A, when challah of chutz la'aretz accidentally mixes with permitted bread, what ratio is needed to permit it?

options.label: B
options.text: 60:1 (shishim).

options.label: C
options.text: Even at a 50-50 ratio, the bread is permitted.

options.label: D
options.text: It can never be permitted.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Distinction #1 – Bitul Ratios
sourceRef.snippet: The S"A in סימן שכג (11) passkens that challah of chutz la'aretz which mixes with permitted bread is allowed even at a 50-50 ratio, and one may deliberately be mevatel challah of CL.

questions.text: According to the Rama, what ratio is needed when challah of chutz la'aretz mixes with the same type of bread (min b'mino)?

options.label: B
options.text: 60:1 (shishim).

options.label: D
options.text: It is always forbidden.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Distinction #1 – Bitul Ratios
sourceRef.snippet: The Rama argues: min b'mino requires 100:1, and min b'she'eino mino requires shishim (60:1). One may not deliberately be mevatel a rabbinic prohibition.

questions.text: Scenario: You live in chutz la'aretz. On Shabbos night, your wife tells you she forgot to be mafrish challah from the challah she baked. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Be mafrish challah immediately – it is permitted on Shabbos in chutz la'aretz.

options.label: B
options.text: You may eat the challah on Shabbos and leave over a small amount to be mafrish from after Shabbos.

options.label: C
options.text: You may not eat the challah at all and must borrow from a neighbor.

options.label: D
options.text: You should be mafrish during bein hashmashos of motzaei Shabbos.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Forgot to Mafrish on Shabbos
sourceRef.snippet: In CL, if you forgot to mafrish, you may eat the challah and leave over a piece. Being mafrish on Shabbos is prohibited because of 'metaken' (fixing), but in CL you have the remedy of eating and leaving over.

questions.text: Same scenario – but now you live in Eretz Yisrael. You forgot to be mafrish challah and it is now Shabbos. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: You may eat the challah and leave over a piece.

options.label: B
options.text: You may be mafrish on Shabbos in Eretz Yisrael since it is a biblical mitzvah.

options.label: C
options.text: You may not eat from the challah and should borrow from a neighbor.

options.label: D
options.text: You should eat the challah and do teshuvah after Shabbos.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Forgot to Mafrish on Shabbos
sourceRef.snippet: In EY, you may not eat from the challah on Shabbos. There is no provision of eating first and being mafrish later. You should borrow challah from a neighbor.

questions.text: If you remember during bein hashmashos on erev Shabbos that you forgot to be mafrish: In chutz la'aretz you may NOT be mafrish, but in Eretz Yisrael you MAY. Why the difference?

options.label: A
options.text: In chutz la'aretz the mitzvah is only rabbinic so there is no urgency.

options.label: B
options.text: In chutz la'aretz there is a remedy on Shabbos itself (eating and leaving over), so being mafrish during bein hashmashos is unnecessary. In Eretz Yisrael, there is no remedy on Shabbos, so Chazal permitted it during bein hashmashos.

options.label: C
options.text: Bein hashmashos in Eretz Yisrael is always considered a weekday.

options.label: D
options.text: The Rama permits being mafrish during bein hashmashos everywhere, but the S"A only permits it in Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Bein Hashmashos
sourceRef.snippet: In CL there is a remedy on Shabbos (eating and leaving over), so Chazal did not permit being mafrish during bein hashmashos. In EY, where there is no remedy, Chazal permitted it during bein hashmashos.

questions.text: A mother in Eretz Yisrael baked challah and sent some to her married children, then remembered on Shabbos that she forgot to be mafrish. According to the Baruch Omer, what should she do?

options.label: A
options.text: She may not do anything until after Shabbos.

options.label: B
options.text: She should be mafrish from the challah she has at home and have it cover what was sent – since it is her fault that her children would eat tevel.

options.label: C
options.text: She should call her children and tell them not to eat the challah.

options.label: D
options.text: She should rely on the fact that her children are transgressing accidentally, so no action is needed.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Sent Challah to Children
sourceRef.snippet: The Baruch Omer holds that since she caused the problem, she may be mafrish on Shabbos to prevent her children from eating tevel. A chaver (knowledgeable person) may violate a lesser rabbinic prohibition to prevent others from sinning.

questions.text: The Nesivos HaMishpat seems to disagree with the previous approach. What is his reasoning?

options.label: A
options.text: Being mafrish on Shabbos is always a biblical violation and can never be permitted.

options.label: B
options.text: One who accidentally transgresses a rabbinic prohibition does not even need to do teshuvah, so the children are not truly sinning, and therefore the mother cannot violate two prohibitions to save them.

options.label: C
options.text: Only a Beis Din may be mafrish from one location to another.

options.label: D
options.text: The mother is not considered a Chaver and therefore may not be mafrish for others.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Nesivos HaMishpat's View
sourceRef.snippet: The Nesivos HaMishpat seems to hold that one who accidentally transgresses a rabbinic prohibition doesn't even need teshuvah. If the children aren't truly sinning, the mother cannot violate two prohibitions (metaken on Shabbos + shelo min hamukaf) to save them.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): In chutz la'aretz, if the dough was divided into multiple pieces, you may be mafrish challah from the loaves in front of you to cover loaves not physically present, as long as they are from the same batch of dough.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Distinction #3 – Shelo Min Hamukaf
sourceRef.snippet: In CL, you may be mafrish from the loaves before you to cover those not present, as long as they are from the same batch of dough. In EY, you should only be mafrish from bread physically before you.

questions.text: What should you do if you forgot to be mafrish challah from your matzah and it is now Pesach night in chutz la'aretz?

options.label: A
options.text: You should be mafrish immediately on Yom Tov since it is a mitzvah.

options.label: B
options.text: You may not eat any matzah the entire Pesach.

options.label: C
options.text: You should leave over a piece from each matzah batch and be mafrish after Yom Tov.

options.label: D
options.text: You may eat freely since matzah is exempt from challah.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Matzah on Pesach (CL)
sourceRef.snippet: In CL, the same rule applies as on Shabbos: you may eat and leave over a piece to be mafrish from after Yom Tov. You must leave over from each batch since you can only be mafrish shelo min hamukaf from the same batch.

questions.text: What are the three solutions suggested for someone in Eretz Yisrael who forgot to be mafrish before Pesach? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: RSZA: If there is no other way to get kosher matzah, one may be mafrish on Pesach to fulfill the biblical mitzvah of eating matzah.

options.label: B
options.text: Chalas Lechem: Give the matzah as a gift to a child (12-year-old boy or 11-year-old girl) and have the child be mafrish.

options.label: C
options.text: RSZA: The Rav of the community should be mafrish before Pesach with intention to cover anyone who forgot.

options.label: D
options.text: One should simply eat the matzah without hafrashas challah since in sha'as hadchak it is permitted.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Matzah on Pesach (EY)
sourceRef.snippet: Three solutions: 1) RSZA: in EY one may be mafrish on Yom Tov to fulfill the biblical mitzvah of eating matzah. 2) Chalas Lechem: give to a 12-year-old boy or 11-year-old girl to be mafrish. 3) RSZA himself would be mafrish before Pesach for his entire community in Shaarei Chessed.

questions.text: RSZA used to be mafrish before Pesach for his entire community in Shaarei Chessed. A glaring question on this practice is:

options.label: A
options.text: A Rav cannot make a bracha on behalf of others.

options.label: B
options.text: How can he be mafrish from one location to another in Eretz Yisrael, and even in chutz la'aretz it doesn't work from one batch to another?

options.label: C
options.text: The matzah may have already been eaten before his hafrasha.

options.label: D
options.text: He was only allowed to do this because he was the Rav.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – RSZA's Practice
sourceRef.snippet: A glaring question: How could RSZA be mafrish shelo min hamukaf in EY? This is normally forbidden. The answer is based on the principle that a chaver may violate a smaller prohibition to prevent the masses from eating tevel.

questions.text: If matzah was made in Eretz Yisrael and sent to chutz la'aretz, and they forgot to be mafrish, the Chashev Ha'Efod quotes the Bach who passkens that:

options.label: A
options.text: The matzah may not be eaten and must be destroyed.

options.label: B
options.text: The matzah has only a rabbinic obligation of challah, so you may eat and leave over a piece to be mafrish from.

options.label: C
options.text: The matzah retains its Eretz Yisrael obligation and may not be eaten before being mafrish.

options.label: D
options.text: The matzah is exempt from challah once it leaves Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Sending Matzah to CL
sourceRef.snippet: The Chashev Ha'Efod quotes the Bach: dough made in EY and sent to CL follows the din of where the dough was made. However, the Bach passkens leniently that it has CL status, so you may eat and leave over.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): Being mafrish challah on Shabbos is prohibited because of "metaken" (fixing).

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Forgot on Shabbos
sourceRef.snippet: Being mafrish on Shabbos is prohibited because of 'metaken' – by separating challah, you 'fix' (permit) the remaining dough, which constitutes a rabbinic violation on Shabbos.

questions.text: According to the Mishnah, a person baking bread at home should separate what fraction of the dough for challah?

sourceRef.section: D. How Much to be Mafrish?
sourceRef.snippet: The Mishnah teaches that a household baker separates 1/24 of the dough, while a commercial baker separates 1/48.

questions.text: A commercial baker only needs to separate 1/48 of the dough. Why is one baking at home required to give more (1/24)?

options.label: A
options.text: Because a household needs to give a more respectable amount, but since a baker makes larger batches, 1/48 of his dough is already roughly the same size as 1/24 of a household batch.

options.label: B
options.text: Because a baker gives challah more frequently, so each time he gives less.

options.label: C
options.text: Because the Torah only requires 1/48 from a baker but mandates 1/24 from a household.

options.label: D
options.text: Because a baker's dough is usually tamei.

sourceRef.section: D. How Much to be Mafrish?
sourceRef.snippet: A household baker gives 1/24 to ensure a respectable amount. A commercial baker makes much larger batches, so even 1/48 yields a similarly sized piece. The household amount ensures dignity for the kohen.

questions.text: The Rambam holds that separating and giving challah is one mitzvah, while the Ramban argues there are two mitzvos. What are the two mitzvos according to the Ramban?

options.label: A
options.text: 1) The mitzvah of baking bread. 2) The mitzvah of giving to the kohen.

options.label: B
options.text: 1) The mitzvah of separating dough (making the rest permissible). 2) The mitzvah of giving dough to the kohen.

options.label: C
options.text: 1) The mitzvah of kneading dough. 2) The mitzvah of burning the challah.

options.label: D
options.text: 1) The mitzvah of separating 1/24. 2) The mitzvah of separating 1/48.

sourceRef.section: D. How Much to be Mafrish? > Rambam vs. Ramban
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam (mitzvah 133) counts it as one mitzvah. The Ramban disagrees and counts two: 1) the mitzvah of separating (making the rest permissible), and 2) the mitzvah of giving the dough to the kohen.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): According to the Rambam, biblically speaking, you may fulfill the mitzvah of hafrashas challah by separating even the smallest amount of dough.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: D. How Much to be Mafrish?
sourceRef.snippet: Min haTorah, even the smallest amount (mashehu) fulfills the obligation. The 1/24 and 1/48 are rabbinic enactments to ensure a respectable gift.

questions.text: How do we passkin lema'ase regarding how much challah to separate today?

options.label: A
options.text: Both Ashkenazim and Sephardim: Separate any amount and burn it; the custom is to separate a kezayis.

options.label: B
options.text: Ashkenazim: 1/24. Sephardim: 1/48.

options.label: C
options.text: Everyone separates exactly 1/24 and gives it to a confirmed kohen.

options.label: D
options.text: The Rama requires 1/10 of the dough to be separated.

sourceRef.section: D. How Much to be Mafrish? > Lema'ase Today
sourceRef.snippet: Today, since we are all tamei and cannot give challah to a kohen, we separate any amount (custom is a kezayis) and burn it. There is no practical difference between 1/24 and 1/48.

questions.text: Why is the challah burned today instead of being given to a kohen?

options.label: A
options.text: Because there are no kohanim alive today.

options.label: B
options.text: Because we are all tamei and the challah is tamei, and we are not certain who is actually a kohen today.

options.label: C
options.text: Because the Torah requires challah to always be burned.

options.label: D
options.text: Because the Rambam prohibited giving challah to kohanim outside of Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah?
sourceRef.snippet: Today, the challah must be burned because we are all assumed to be tamei (ritually impure), and we cannot confirm with certainty who is a kohen. Therefore the challah cannot be eaten and must be destroyed.

questions.text: When burning challah in your home oven, why should the challah be double-wrapped in tinfoil?

options.label: A
options.text: Because the bracha requires the challah to be wrapped.

options.label: B
options.text: To prevent issues of reicha (aroma) or zei'ah (steam) that may forbid other food in the oven.

options.label: C
options.text: Because a non-kohen may not see the challah being burned.

options.label: D
options.text: To ensure the challah is fully incinerated.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Practical Application
sourceRef.snippet: Double-wrap in tinfoil to prevent issues of reicha (aroma) and zei'ah (steam) that could forbid other food baking simultaneously in the oven.

questions.text: If the tinfoil around the challah ripped while baking in the oven with other food, what is the status of the other food?

options.label: A
options.text: The other food is forbidden and must be discarded.

options.label: B
options.text: The other food is permitted because bedieved we do not take reicha into consideration, and dry challah does not emit steam.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the food directly touching the challah is forbidden.

options.label: D
options.text: The entire oven needs to be kashered.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Practical Application – Tinfoil Ripped
sourceRef.snippet: Bedieved, if the tinfoil ripped: reicha is not a concern bedieved, and dry challah does not emit zei'ah (steam). Therefore the other food in the oven is permitted.

questions.text: Why should you not stick a fork into the challah to check if it is fully burnt?

options.label: A
options.text: Because the challah might still have kedusha.

options.label: B
options.text: Because if the challah isn't burned through, the forbidden challah would render the fork non-kosher, requiring libun.

options.label: C
options.text: Because you may not touch challah with a metal utensil.

options.label: D
options.text: Because it is a disgrace to the mitzvah.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Practical Application – Fork Issue
sourceRef.snippet: Do not stick a fork into the burning challah – if it isn't fully burned through, the forbidden challah renders the fork non-kosher, requiring libun (direct-fire kashering).

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): If you have no way to burn the challah, Rav Ovadya passkens that you should separate less than a kezayis, wrap it in a bag, and dispose of it.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Alternative Disposal
sourceRef.snippet: If there is no way to burn the challah, Rav Ovadya passkens: separate less than a kezayis, wrap it in a bag, and dispose of it.

questions.text: What does the Chalas Kehilchasa passkin about when you must burn the challah?

options.label: A
options.text: You may hold on to it and burn it whenever convenient.

options.label: B
options.text: The challah should be burned right away to avoid accidentally benefiting from it.

options.label: C
options.text: It should be frozen until erev Pesach and burned with the chametz.

options.label: D
options.text: It only needs to be burned before the next Shabbos.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Timing
sourceRef.snippet: The Chalas Kehilchasa passkens that the challah should be burned right away to avoid the concern of accidentally benefiting from it (hana'ah) or it getting mixed back into permitted food.

questions.text: Which of the following are concerns when burning challah on the stovetop directly on the grates? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: The grates will get dirty.

options.label: B
options.text: The grates may become non-kosher since today's grates are elevated above the fire and the flames may not reach them fully.

options.label: C
options.text: Burning on the stovetop is rabbinically prohibited.

options.label: D
options.text: The fire will be extinguished.

sourceRef.section: E. What To Do with the Separated Challah? > Practical Application – Stovetop
sourceRef.snippet: On today's stovetops, the grates are elevated above the fire. The concerns: 1) the grates get dirty from the burning challah, and 2) the forbidden challah may render the grates non-kosher since the flames may not fully reach them for libun.

questions.text: Which of the following grains are obligated in hafrashas challah? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Wheat, barley, spelt.

options.label: B
options.text: Oats and rye.

options.label: C
options.text: Rice and corn.

options.label: D
options.text: All of the above.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Five Grains
sourceRef.snippet: Only the five grains are obligated in hafrashas challah: wheat, barley, spelt, oats, and rye. Rice, corn, and other grains are exempt.

questions.text: The Gemara teaches that one is only obligated to be mafrish challah from a batch of dough the size of the Manna in the midbar (an omer). How much is an omer?

options.label: A
options.text: 1/3 of an eifah.

options.label: B
options.text: 1/10 of an eifah.

options.label: C
options.text: 1/6 of an eifah.

options.label: D
options.text: 1 eifah.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Size of an Omer
sourceRef.snippet: The Torah says 'arisoseichem' – your dough. The Gemara derives the minimum size from the Manna: one omer per person, which equals 1/10 of an eifah.

questions.text: The acronym כאסקלב יגודו is used to calculate halachic measurements. What does it represent?

options.label: A
options.text: The names of the Rishonim who discuss the measurements.

options.label: B
options.text: כאסקלב = the units (כור, איפה, סאה, קב, לוג, ביצה) and יגודו = the numerical ratios between them (10, 3, 6, 4, 6).

options.label: C
options.text: The steps for performing hafrashas challah.

options.label: D
options.text: The names of the five grains.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Calculating the Measurements
sourceRef.snippet: כאסקלב represents the measurement units: כור, איפה, סאה, קב, לוג, ביצה. יגודו represents the conversion ratios: 10 (כור→איפה not used here), 3 (איפה→סאה), 6 (סאה→קב), 4 (קב→לוג), 6 (לוג→ביצה).

questions.text: Using the כאסקלב system, how many eggs are in 1/10 of an eifah?

options.label: A
options.text: 24 eggs.

options.label: B
options.text: 36 eggs.

options.label: D
options.text: 60 eggs.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Egg Calculation
sourceRef.snippet: 1/10 eifah = 1/10 of (3 se'ah × 6 kav × 4 log × 6 eggs) = 1/10 of 432 eggs = 43.2 eggs (43 and 1/5 eggs).

questions.text: The Tzlach (Rav Yechezkel Landau) discovered a major problem with the two Gemara-based calculations for the volume of challah (eggs vs. cubic thumb widths). What did he find?

options.label: A
options.text: The egg calculation was exactly double the thumb calculation.

options.label: B
options.text: The thumb-width calculation came out to almost double the volume of the egg calculation.

options.label: C
options.text: The two calculations were identical.

options.label: D
options.text: The egg calculation depends on whether you use chicken or goose eggs.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > The Tzlach's Contradiction
sourceRef.snippet: The Tzlach found that Gemara Pesachim's thumb-width calculation (311 cubic godel) yields almost double the volume of the 43.2 eggs calculation. This is a major contradiction between two Gemara-based measurements.

questions.text: How did the Tzlach resolve this discrepancy?

options.label: A
options.text: People's thumbs have gotten smaller over time.

options.label: B
options.text: The eggs in Chazal's time were approximately half the size of today's eggs.

options.label: C
options.text: Today's eggs are approximately half the size of those in Chazal's time.

options.label: D
options.text: The Gemara's measurements were only approximate.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > The Tzlach's Resolution
sourceRef.snippet: The Tzlach concluded that eggs must have shrunk to approximately half their original size since Chazal's time. Therefore, today's 43.2 eggs represent only half the volume that Chazal intended, and one should use approximately 86 of today's eggs.

questions.text: You have 1.2 kg of flour and are making challah. What should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Be mafrish challah with a bracha.

options.label: B
options.text: Be mafrish challah without a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: No need to be mafrish at all.

options.label: D
options.text: Only be mafrish if you live in Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Weight Thresholds
sourceRef.snippet: At 1.2 kg, all opinions agree you should be mafrish, but without a bracha. This is based on the SSK's lower threshold where one should begin separating challah.

questions.text: You are using 1.56 kg of flour. According to Rav Ovadya Yosef, should you recite a bracha when being mafrish?

options.label: A
options.text: No bracha – this is below the threshold.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes – Rav Ovadya passkens that 1.56 kg requires mafrish with a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: Only with a bracha if in Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: D
options.text: Rav Ovadya does not discuss this amount.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Rav Ovadya's Position
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Ovadya Yosef holds that 1.56 kg (based on the Rambam's Egyptian dirham calculation: 520 dirhams × 3g = 1560g) is the threshold for mafrish with a bracha.

questions.text: According to Rav Chaim Naeh, at what weight of flour should you be mafrish with a bracha?

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Rav Chaim Naeh's Position
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Chaim Naeh: each egg = 57.6 cc, so 43.2 eggs = 2488 cc. Using packed flour's weight ratio (about 1/3 less than water), the weight is 1.66 kg. RSZA, Rav Wosner, and the Yerushalayim custom follow this.

questions.text: According to the Chazon Ish, at what weight of flour do you recite a bracha when being mafrish?

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Chazon Ish's Position
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish follows the Tzlach: eggs shrank to half, so the volume is doubled (4320 cc). With flour at 52% the weight of water, the threshold is 2.25 kg. The Steipler and Rav Chaim Kanievsky follow this.

questions.text: Match the correct weight to the correct authority. Which of the following pairings are correct? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: 1.2 kg – all opinions agree to be mafrish without a bracha.

options.label: B
options.text: 1.56 kg – Rav Ovadya: mafrish with a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: 1.66 kg – Chazon Ish: mafrish with a bracha.

options.label: D
options.text: 2.25 kg – Chazon Ish: mafrish with a bracha.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Summary of Thresholds
sourceRef.snippet: 1.2 kg: all agree, no bracha. 1.56 kg: Rav Ovadya says with bracha. 1.66 kg: Rav Chaim Naeh says with bracha (not the Chazon Ish). 2.25 kg: Chazon Ish says with bracha.

questions.text: Rav Chaim Naeh reconciled the Tzlach's discrepancy differently. How?

options.label: A
options.text: He proved that eggs have actually grown bigger since Chazal's time.

options.label: B
options.text: He argued that the thumb width should be measured from the narrow section, not the wide section, which brings the two calculations much closer together.

options.label: C
options.text: He rejected the thumb-width calculation entirely.

options.label: D
options.text: He showed that the Gemara in Pesachim was referring to a different type of measurement.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Rav Chaim Naeh's Reconciliation
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Chaim Naeh argued that the godel (thumb width) should be measured from the narrow section (~2 cm) rather than the wide section. When measured this way, the thumb calculation aligns closely with the egg calculation, eliminating the need to say eggs shrank.

questions.text: How does the Biur Halacha passkin regarding the Tzlach / Rav Chaim Naeh dispute?

options.label: A
options.text: Always follow the Tzlach's larger measurement.

options.label: B
options.text: Always follow Rav Chaim Naeh's smaller measurement.

options.label: C
options.text: For biblical mitzvos use the larger Tzlach measurement; for rabbinic mitzvos use the smaller Rav Chaim Naeh measurement.

options.label: D
options.text: Follow whichever measurement your community traditionally uses.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Biur Halacha's Ruling
sourceRef.snippet: The Biur Halacha passkens: for biblical mitzvos, use the larger Tzlach measurement (lechumra); for rabbinic mitzvos, the smaller Rav Chaim Naeh measurement suffices. Since challah today is rabbinic, 43.2 of today's eggs (Rav Chaim Naeh) is enough.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): The weight required for hafrashas challah (e.g., 1.66 kg) refers to the flour only, and does not include the added water, sugar, and eggs.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Flour Weight Only
sourceRef.snippet: The required weight refers to the flour only — water, sugar, eggs, and other additives are not included in the calculation.

questions.text: When using whole wheat flour where the bran was removed during milling and added back later, what adjustment to the weight is needed?

options.label: A
options.text: No adjustment is needed – whole wheat flour is measured the same as white flour.

options.label: B
options.text: You need to add at least 15% more flour, since the bran that was added back may not be included in the measurement.

options.label: C
options.text: You should subtract 15% since bran takes up more volume.

options.label: D
options.text: You should use volume measurement instead of weight for whole wheat.

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Whole Wheat Flour
sourceRef.snippet: In commercial whole wheat flour, the bran is typically removed and added back. The re-added bran may not be halachically part of the flour, so add at least 15% more to ensure the actual flour content reaches the required weight.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): If the bran was NOT removed during milling (as in smaller organic mills), the required weight is actually LESS than 1.66 kg because bran takes up more volume but weighs less.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Organic Whole Wheat
sourceRef.snippet: If the bran was never removed (smaller organic mills), the flour naturally contains bran which takes up more volume but weighs less per unit volume. Therefore you may need less weight to reach the required volume.

questions.text: When using frozen flour, the Levush Mordechai writes that you should:

options.label: A
options.text: Add a bit more flour to the required weight, because frozen flour weighs more due to frozen condensation.

options.label: B
options.text: Use less flour, since freezing makes the flour more dense.

options.label: C
options.text: Thaw the flour for exactly 24 hours before measuring.

options.label: D
options.text: Never use frozen flour for challah.

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Frozen Flour
sourceRef.snippet: The Levush Mordechai: frozen flour absorbs condensation which adds weight. Add a bit more flour to compensate, so the actual dry flour content reaches the shiur.

questions.text: You made a batch of dough with 1.3 kg of flour and were mafrish without a bracha. You then added more flour bringing the total to 1.8 kg. What is the problem?

options.label: A
options.text: There is no problem – the original hafrasha covers the entire batch.

options.label: B
options.text: You are stuck: if 1.2 kg is the real threshold, the hafrasha already worked and you don't need to be mafrish again; but if 1.66 kg is the real threshold, you now need to be mafrish with a bracha for the first time.

options.label: C
options.text: You must throw out the entire batch and start over.

options.label: D
options.text: You should be mafrish again with a bracha on the additional flour only.

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Adding More Flour
sourceRef.snippet: Problem: If 1.2 kg is the correct shiur, the first hafrasha worked — being mafrish again is bracha levatallah. If 1.66 kg is the shiur, you must now be mafrish with a bracha. This creates a safek bracha dilemma.

questions.text: According to the Chalas Kehilchasa, if you started with 1.3 kg (mafrish without a bracha) and then added another 1.3 kg (total 2.6 kg), what should you do?

options.label: A
options.text: No need to do anything – the original hafrasha covers everything.

options.label: B
options.text: Be mafrish again without a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: Be mafrish again with a bracha – because regardless of whether the threshold is 1.2 or 1.66, both opinions would now require a bracha.

options.label: D
options.text: Wait 24 hours and then be mafrish with a bracha.

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Adding More Flour (Large Amount)
sourceRef.snippet: If the total exceeds all thresholds (e.g., 2.6 kg > 2.25 kg Chazon Ish), you can be mafrish again with a bracha. If the first hafrasha was valid, this second one is extra but still a valid bracha since the dough now independently requires it.

questions.text: Is there an obligation to be mafrish challah on gluten-free wheat flour (wheat starch)?

options.label: A
options.text: No – since it cannot rise and become chametz, it is completely exempt.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes with a bracha – according to Rav Rubin it is considered flour from the 5 grains and is obligated.

options.label: C
options.text: Most poskim say to be mafrish without a bracha due to safek; Rav Rubin passkens mafrish with a bracha if it was made by a Jew.

options.label: D
options.text: Only the Chazon Ish requires hafrasha; everyone else exempts it.

sourceRef.section: G. Practical Application > Gluten-Free Wheat Flour
sourceRef.snippet: Most poskim: be mafrish without a bracha due to safek. Rav Rubin distinguishes: if the wheat starch was produced by a non-Jew, be mafrish without a bracha; if produced by a Jew, be mafrish with a bracha since it retains the status of flour from the 5 grains.

questions.text: When different types of flour are mixed together BEFORE water is added, which grains can combine to reach the shiur for hafrashas challah?

options.label: A
options.text: Only wheat and spelt can combine.

options.label: B
options.text: Only grains that are not kilayim with each other.

options.label: C
options.text: All five grains can combine with each other.

options.label: D
options.text: No grains can combine – each must reach the shiur independently.

sourceRef.section: H. Combining Flour from Different Grains > Scenario 1 – Mixed Before Water
sourceRef.snippet: When different grains are mixed in flour form before becoming dough (or kneaded together), all five grains combine with each other to reach the shiur. This is based on Mishnah Challah 1:1.

questions.text: When two separate batches of dough (each below the shiur) are touching, which grain can combine with ALL other grains?

options.label: A
options.text: Wheat.

options.label: B
options.text: Barley.

options.label: C
options.text: Spelt.

options.label: D
options.text: Oats.

sourceRef.section: H. Combining Flour from Different Grains > Scenario 2 – Touching Batches
sourceRef.snippet: When two separate batches are touching, each below the shiur, only specific pairings combine. Spelt is unique — it can combine with ALL other grains. Wheat only combines with itself and spelt.

questions.text: When two separate batches of dough are touching (each below the shiur), wheat can combine with which grain(s)? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Wheat.

options.label: B
options.text: Spelt.

options.label: C
options.text: Barley.

options.label: D
options.text: Oats.

sourceRef.section: H. Combining Flour from Different Grains > Scenario 2 – Wheat Pairings
sourceRef.snippet: Wheat can only combine with itself and spelt when the two separate batches are touching. It cannot combine with barley, oats, or rye in this scenario.

questions.text: When two separate batches each have a shiur of challah independently, you may be mafrish from one to the other only if:

options.label: A
options.text: They are from any of the 5 grains – all combine.

options.label: B
options.text: They are from the same grain, OR from spelt to rye (and vice versa) and from barley to oats (and vice versa).

options.label: C
options.text: They are from the same grain only.

options.label: D
options.text: They are placed in the same container.

sourceRef.section: H. Combining Flour from Different Grains > Scenario 3 – Each Has Shiur
sourceRef.snippet: When each batch independently has a shiur: you may only be mafrish from one for the other if they are from the same grain, or from spelt↔rye and barley↔oats, based on Mishnah Terumos 2:4 and Kilayim 1:1.

questions.text: Explain the dispute between the Tzlach and Rav Chaim Naeh regarding the calculation of halachic measurements. What contradiction did the Tzlach find, how did he resolve it, and how did Rav Chaim Naeh disagree? Include what the Biur Halacha passkens lema'ase.
questions.modelAnswer: The Tzlach discovered that the two Gemara-based calculations for determining the volume of challah (the egg-based calculation of 43.2 eggs and the thumb-width-based calculation of 311 cubic thumb widths) produced drastically different results – the thumb calculation came out to almost double the egg calculation. He concluded that eggs must have shrunk to half their original size since Chazal's time. Therefore, to be mafrish with today's eggs, you'd need about 86 eggs. The Chazon Ish agreed with this approach. Rav Chaim Naeh argued that eggs did not shrink; rather, the thumb width should be measured from the narrow section of the thumb (about 2cm) rather than the wider section. When measured this way, the two calculations align. The Biur Halacha passkens that for biblical mitzvos one should use the larger Tzlach measurement, and for rabbinic mitzvos the smaller Rav Chaim Naeh measurement may be used. Since hafrashas challah today is rabbinic, 43.2 of today's eggs (the Rav Chaim Naeh measurement) suffices.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Tzlach's Contradiction & Reconciliations
sourceRef.snippet: Two Gemara calculations yield different volumes. Tzlach: eggs shrank to half. R' Chaim Naeh: measure thumb from narrow section. Biur Halacha: biblical = large (Tzlach), rabbinic = small (R' Chaim Naeh).

questions.text: Describe the three main halachic differences between challah in Eretz Yisrael and challah in chutz la'aretz. For each difference, provide a practical example of when this would make a difference.
questions.modelAnswer: 1) Bitul ratios: In chutz la'aretz, S"A permits challah mixed with bread even at 50-50; Rama requires 100:1 (min b'mino) or 60:1 (min b'she'eino mino). In EY, one may not deliberately be mevatel. Practical example: If separated challah accidentally fell back into the dough in chutz la'aretz, it's easily nullified. 2) Eating before being mafrish: In chutz la'aretz, if you forgot, you may eat the bread and leave over a small piece to be mafrish from later. In EY, you may NOT eat before being mafrish. Practical example: If on Shabbos you forgot to be mafrish – in chutz la'aretz you eat and leave over; in EY you must borrow from a neighbor. 3) Hafrasha shelo min hamukaf: In chutz la'aretz, you may be mafrish from bread before you to cover bread not present, as long as it's from the same batch. In EY, you should only be mafrish from bread that is in front of you. Practical example: If you sent loaves to another location and they're from the same batch, in chutz la'aretz you can be mafrish from your loaves to cover the sent ones; in EY you should not.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences Between Challah of EY and Chutz La'aretz
sourceRef.snippet: Three distinctions: 1) bitul ratios, 2) eating before mafrish, 3) shelo min hamukaf. Each is more lenient in CL because its challah was only enacted to preserve the mitzvah, while EY challah is biblical in nature.

questions.text: Explain why the weight required for hafrashas challah varies so much between poskim (1.2 kg, 1.56 kg, 1.66 kg, 2.25 kg). Include the reasoning behind each number and which authority holds each position.
questions.modelAnswer: The variation stems from how the volume of 43.2 eggs is converted to a weight, and whether the eggs of Chazal's time were the same size as today's. 1.2 kg – All agree to be mafrish without a bracha. This corresponds to the volume of 43.2 modern eggs converted to flour weight using Rav Chaim Naeh's smaller egg volume. The SSK uses this as the lower bound. 1.56 kg – Rav Ovadya Yosef – based on the Rambam who converts the volume to 520 Egyptian dirhams. A talmid chachom traveled to Egypt and found that dirhams never weighed more than 3 grams, so 520 × 3g = 1560g. 1.66 kg – Rav Chaim Naeh – who calculated each egg today as 57.6 cc, making 43.2 eggs = 2488 cc. Using packed flour's weight ratio (1/3 less than water), the weight comes to 1.66 kg. RSZA, Rav Wosner, and the custom in Yerushalayim follow this. 2.25 kg – Chazon Ish – who follows the Tzlach that eggs shrank to half their original size. Therefore the volume is doubled (4320 cc) and with flour weighing 52% of water = 2.25 kg. The Steipler and Rav Chaim Kanievsky followed this, and it is the common practice in chutz la'aretz where people use 5 lbs or more.

sourceRef.section: F. Amount of Dough Needed > Weight Thresholds Summary
sourceRef.snippet: Four thresholds based on different calculations: 1.2 kg (SSK lower bound), 1.56 kg (Rav Ovadya via Rambam/dirhams), 1.66 kg (R' Chaim Naeh via egg volume), 2.25 kg (Chazon Ish via Tzlach's doubled eggs).

questions.text: A family in Eretz Yisrael forgot to be mafrish challah from their matzah before Pesach. It is now Pesach night. Discuss the three solutions suggested by the poskim for how they can eat their matzah on the Seder night.
questions.modelAnswer: 1) RSZA (quoted in Shemiras Shabbos Kehilchasa): In EY, if there is no other way to get kosher matzah, one may be mafrish on Pesach in order to fulfill the biblical mitzvah of eating matzah. It is best to do so mentally without a bracha. This is because we do not push off a biblical mitzvah (eating matzah) for a rabbinic concern (metaken on Yom Tov). 2) Chalas Lechem: Based on two halachos – a) a child may violate a derabanan for his own sake (Biur Halacha), and b) a katan whose oaths are binding can perform valid terumah (Rambam). Therefore, give the matzah as a gift to a child (12-year-old boy or 11-year-old girl) and have them be mafrish. Since their hafrasha is valid, everyone else can benefit from the matzah. 3) RSZA himself used to be mafrish before Pesach on behalf of his entire community in Shaarei Chessed, having in mind all who might forget. This works based on the principle that a Chaver may violate a smaller prohibition (hafrasha from one location to another) to prevent the masses from transgressing the greater prohibition of eating tevel. However, the Chakat HaPesach cautions that if the Rav is mafrish in the morning and someone else is mafrish later in the day, the Rav's hafrasha is no longer relevant, and the later person may recite a bracha levatallah.

sourceRef.section: C. Differences > Practical Applications – Matzah on Pesach (EY)
sourceRef.snippet: Three solutions: 1) RSZA permits mafrish on Pesach for matzah obligation. 2) Chalas Lechem: give to child (boy 12/girl 11) to be mafrish. 3) RSZA was pre-mafrish for entire community. Chakat HaPesach warns about bracha levatallah if another later is mafrish.

questions.text: Describe the three scenarios of combining different grains for hafrashas challah. For each scenario, explain the halachic rule and give examples of which grains can or cannot combine.
questions.modelAnswer: There are three distinct scenarios: 1) Grains mixed in flour form (before becoming dough, or kneaded together after): ALL five grains combine with each other. For example, if you mix 800g of wheat flour with 500g of spelt flour before adding water, and the total exceeds the shiur, you are obligated. This is based on Mishnah Challah 1:1 which teaches all grains combine. 2) Two separate batches of dough, each below the shiur, touching each other: Only specific pairings combine. Spelt is unique – it combines with ALL other grains. Wheat only combines with itself and spelt. Barley combines with everything except wheat. Rye combines with everything except wheat and oats. Oats combine with everything except wheat and rye. This is based on Mishnah Challah 4:2. 3) Two separate batches, each independently at the shiur of challah: You may only be mafrish from one for the other if they are from the same grain, OR from spelt to rye (and vice versa), OR from barley to oats (and vice versa) – because these are the pairings that are not kilayim with each other (Mishnah Terumos 2:4 and Kilayim 1:1).

sourceRef.section: H. Combining Flour from Different Grains
sourceRef.snippet: Three scenarios: 1) Mixed as flour → all 5 combine. 2) Separate doughs touching → only specific pairings (spelt combines with all). 3) Each has shiur → only same grain or non-kilayim pairs (spelt↔rye, barley↔oats).

questions.text: The Midrash Tanchuma teaches that one who eats tevel (produce from which tu"m has not been separated) is considered as if he ate:

options.label: A
options.text: Chametz on Pesach.

options.label: B
options.text: Treif.

options.label: C
options.text: On Yom Kippur.

options.label: D
options.text: Kodshim outside the Beis HaMikdash.

sourceRef.section: TUM-A. Introduction to Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Midrash Tanchuma (Re'eh 17) teaches that one who eats tevel, it is like eating treif.

questions.text: The Aderes writes that one who learns the halachos of tu"m, even if he lives in chutz la'aretz, it is as if he:

options.label: A
options.text: Visited Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: B
options.text: Was mafrish tu"m.

options.label: C
options.text: Built the Beis HaMikdash.

options.label: D
options.text: Ate ma'aser sheini in Yerushalayim.

sourceRef.section: TUM-A. Introduction to Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The No'am Halacha quotes the Aderes who writes that just as learning hilchos korban chatas is as if he offered it, so too learning tu"m is as if he was mafrish tu"m.

questions.text: The Gemara Ta'anis learns from the passuk "aser te'aser" that properly giving tu"m is a segulah for:

options.label: A
options.text: Long life.

options.label: B
options.text: Many children.

options.label: C
options.text: Wealth (parnassah).

options.label: D
options.text: Entering Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: TUM-A. Introduction to Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Gemara Ta'anis (9a) learns from 'aser te'aser' (Devarim 14:22) that one becomes wealthy by properly giving tu"m (aser bishvil she'tisasher).

questions.text: Terumah gedolah is given to the kohen. According to the Rambam, who decides which kohen receives the terumah?

options.label: A
options.text: The Beis Din.

options.label: B
options.text: The kohen gadol.

options.label: C
options.text: The Yisrael owner of the produce – he may give it to whichever kohen he prefers.

options.label: D
options.text: It is distributed equally among all kohanim in the city.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam (Terumos 12:15) passkens that the Yisrael owner has the right to give the terumah to whichever kohen he prefers.

questions.text: Biblically, the minimum amount of terumah gedolah that must be given is:

options.label: A
options.text: 1/40 of the produce.

options.label: B
options.text: 1/50 of the produce.

options.label: C
options.text: 1/60 of the produce.

options.label: D
options.text: Any amount, even the smallest amount.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam (Terumos 3:1-2) teaches that biblically one can fulfill his obligation by giving any amount, even the smallest amount. However, Chazal gave gradations: generous = 1/40, average = 1/50, stingy = 1/60.

questions.text: According to Chazal, a person with a generous eye gives what fraction of his produce as terumah gedolah?

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Chazal gave gradations: a generous person gives 1/40 (2.5%), an average person gives 1/50 (2%), and a stingy person gives 1/60 (1.6%).

questions.text: Ma'aser rishon is given to the levi. What is the required amount?

options.label: A
options.text: 10% of the total produce.

options.label: B
options.text: 10% of the remaining produce after terumah gedolah is separated.

options.label: C
options.text: 1% of the total produce.

options.label: D
options.text: Any amount the owner wishes.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Tosfos in Sukkah (23b) explains that the 10% comes off the remaining produce after the terumah gedolah is given to the kohen (not 10% of the total).

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): When Ezra returned to Eretz Yisrael and none of the levi'im came along, he fined them by giving the ma'aser rishon to kohanim instead.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Since the ma'aser rishon has no kedusha and can be eaten by anyone, when Ezra came back and none of the levi'im came along, he fined them and gave the ma'aser rishon to kohanim.

questions.text: Terumas ma'aser is separated from ma'aser rishon. What percentage of the original produce (after terumah gedolah) does terumas ma'aser represent?

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The kohen receives 10% of the 10% separated for the levi, or 1% of all the owner's produce (after terumah gedolah was taken).

questions.text: The Rambam teaches that there is a biblical commandment to separate tu"m in a specific order. What is the correct order?

options.label: A
options.text: Ma'aser rishon → Terumah gedolah → Terumas ma'aser → Ma'aser sheini/ani.

options.label: B
options.text: Terumah gedolah → Ma'aser rishon → Ma'aser sheini/ani.

options.label: C
options.text: Ma'aser sheini → Terumah gedolah → Ma'aser rishon.

options.label: D
options.text: Terumah gedolah → Ma'aser rishon → Terumas ma'aser → Ma'aser sheini/ani.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam (Terumos 3:23) teaches that there is a biblical commandment to separate in order: First terumah gedolah, followed by ma'aser rishon, and then either ma'aser sheini or ma'aser ani.

questions.text: According to the Tosfos quoting the Sefri, what is the purpose of ma'aser sheini?

options.label: A
options.text: To support the kohanim financially.

options.label: B
options.text: To ensure that one spends time in Yerushalayim seeing kohanim and levi'im dedicated to avodas Hashem, which will inspire Torah learning and yiras shamayim.

options.label: C
options.text: To provide food for the poor.

options.label: D
options.text: To ensure that produce is not wasted.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Tosfos in Bava Basra (21a) quotes the Sefri: the purpose of ma'aser sheini is for one to spend time in Yerushalayim seeing kohanim and levi'im dedicated to avodas Hashem, inspiring Torah learning and yiras shamayim.

questions.text: When one redeems his own ma'aser sheini, he must add a chomesh (20%). How is this 20% calculated?

options.label: A
options.text: 20% of the original value – e.g., fruit worth 100 shekel is redeemed for 120.

options.label: B
options.text: 20% calculated after the amount is added (chomesh milbar) – e.g., fruit worth 100 shekel is redeemed for 125.

options.label: C
options.text: An additional 25% of the fruit's value.

options.label: D
options.text: Both B and C are correct descriptions of the same calculation.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The chomesh is calculated after the amount is added (chomesh milbar). If your fruits are worth 100 shekel, you need to redeem for 125 shekel, where 20% of 125 is 25. This is equivalent to adding 25% of the original value.

questions.text: On which years of the shemittah cycle is ma'aser sheini separated? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Years 1 and 2.

options.label: B
options.text: Year 3.

options.label: C
options.text: Years 4 and 5.

options.label: D
options.text: Year 6.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Ma'aser sheini is given on years 1, 2, 4 and 5. Ma'aser ani is given on years 3 and 6. Only one of the two is given in any single year.

questions.text: The Sefri derives from the words "shenas hama'aser" that:

options.label: A
options.text: Ma'aser must be separated every year without exception.

options.label: B
options.text: Only one of the two ma'asros (ma'aser sheini or ma'aser ani) can be given in a single year, not both.

options.label: C
options.text: Ma'aser sheini and ma'aser ani should both be given every year.

options.label: D
options.text: Ma'aser is only given during shemittah.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: The Sefri (Devarim 57) learns from 'shenas hama'aser' that only one of the two ma'asros, ma'aser sheini or ma'aser ani, can fall out on a single year.

questions.text: What are the two main limitations that prevent us from separating tu"m in the manner described in the Torah? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Today we assume everyone is tamei, so terumah, terumas ma'aser and ma'aser sheini cannot be eaten.

options.label: B
options.text: We can no longer confidently identify genuine kohanim due to the length of the exile.

options.label: C
options.text: The Torah only requires tu"m when there is a king.

options.label: D
options.text: Tu"m can only be separated in the Beis HaMikdash.

sourceRef.section: TUM-C. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — Preparations
sourceRef.snippet: Two limitations: 1) Today everyone is assumed tamei. 2) The Maharashal writes that the lineage of the majority of kohanim has been mingled and we can no longer confidently identify genuine kohanim.

questions.text: For the hafrasha of tu"m to take effect (be chal), two conditions must be met. What are they?

options.label: A
options.text: You must recite a bracha and then burn the terumah.

options.label: B
options.text: You must verbally proclaim the produce as terumah/ma'aser (koreh shem) and identify its location in the pile.

options.label: C
options.text: You must give the produce to a kohen and have him eat it.

options.label: D
options.text: You must bring the produce to the Beis HaMikdash and make a declaration.

sourceRef.section: TUM-C. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — Preparations
sourceRef.snippet: The tu"m goes into effect once you identified its location in the pile and proclaimed it to be terumah or ma'aser. The bracha is recited before the proclamation because the proclamation is the actual mitzvah.

questions.text: The Rama passkens that before being mafrish tu"m, you should wet the fruits. Why?

options.label: A
options.text: To make them easier to separate.

options.label: B
options.text: So the fruits become tamei before the hafrasha – otherwise if the terumah is tahor, you can't throw it out (because it's tahor) and you can't give it to a kohen (because he is tamei).

options.label: C
options.text: To ensure the bracha is valid.

options.label: D
options.text: Because dry fruit is exempt from tu"m.

sourceRef.section: TUM-C. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — Preparations
sourceRef.snippet: The Rama (siman 331:19) passkens that you must wet the fruits before hafrasha so they become tamei. Otherwise you're stuck: you can't throw out tahor terumah and you can't give it to a tamei kohen.

questions.text: Before reciting the nussach of the hafrasha, you need to set aside a portion of the produce. How much should you set aside?

options.label: A
options.text: Exactly 10% for ma'aser rishon.

options.label: B
options.text: 1% of all the produce, plus a bit more.

options.label: C
options.text: 1/24 of the produce.

options.label: D
options.text: Half of the produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-C. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — Preparations
sourceRef.snippet: Set aside 1% plus a bit more. The 1% will be terumas ma'aser and the bit more will be terumah gedolah. These are separated from the rest because they may not be eaten.

questions.text: When saying the nussach for hafrashas tu"m today, you only recite a bracha if:

options.label: A
options.text: You are in Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: B
options.text: You are certain the produce before you is tevel (e.g., you took it from your own tree).

options.label: C
options.text: The produce is worth more than a perutah.

options.label: D
options.text: You are a kohen or levi.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: You will only recite a bracha if you are certain the produce is tevel (e.g., you took them off the tree in your house). The bracha is: 'Baruch... l'hafrish terumos u'ma'asros.'

questions.text: In the nussach of terumah gedolah, you say: "The amount that is greater than 1%, situated on the north side, will be terumah gedolah." What happens to the 1% itself at this point?

options.label: A
options.text: It also becomes terumah gedolah.

options.label: B
options.text: It becomes ma'aser rishon.

options.label: C
options.text: It is not yet designated as anything.

options.label: D
options.text: It is immediately destroyed.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: By reciting 'the amount greater than 1% on the north will be terumah gedolah,' you proclaimed the small extra amount as terumah gedolah. In the meantime, the 1% is not designated as anything.

questions.text: In the nussach for ma'aser rishon, you designate the 1% already set aside plus another 9% on the north. Together, these 10% become ma'aser rishon. What happens next to the 1%?

options.label: A
options.text: It is given to the levi along with the other 9%.

options.label: B
options.text: It is proclaimed as terumas ma'aser and given to a kohen.

options.label: C
options.text: It is burned.

options.label: D
options.text: It is eaten by the owner.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: After designating ma'aser rishon, the 1% already set aside is then proclaimed as terumas ma'aser: 'The 1% which I made into ma'aser rishon will now become terumas ma'aser.'

questions.text: The Chazon Ish holds that we should not give terumah to kohanim or ma'aser rishon to a levi today because:

options.label: A
options.text: There is no mitzvah to give tu"m today.

options.label: B
options.text: We cannot be certain that today's kohanim and levi'im possess an unbroken and reliable lineage.

options.label: C
options.text: The levi'im were fined by Ezra and may never receive ma'aser again.

options.label: D
options.text: Only the Beis Din may distribute tu"m.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: The Derech Emunah quotes the Chazon Ish: we do not give terumah to kohanim or ma'aser rishon to levi'im because we cannot be certain of their unbroken lineage. Chazal were stringent with tu"m since many might falsely claim to be kohanim.

questions.text: In Eretz Yisrael, it is customary to formally gift animals in certain zoos to kohanim and feed them tamei tu"m. Where is this practiced?

options.label: A
options.text: The Biblical Zoo in Jerusalem.

options.label: B
options.text: The Ramat Gan Safari.

options.label: C
options.text: The Tel Aviv Zoo.

options.label: D
options.text: This practice does not exist today.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: At the Biblical Zoo in Jerusalem, the animals are officially owned by kohanim, and farmers send their tu"m there rather than discarding it, fulfilling the mitzvah of giving terumah to a kohen.

questions.text: There is a dispute between the Chazon Ish and RSZA regarding redeeming ma'aser sheini on a coin. The Chazon Ish holds that the fruit and the chomesh should be redeemed on:

options.label: A
options.text: A single perutah that covers both.

options.label: B
options.text: A perutah for the fruit, plus an additional quarter perutah for the chomesh – because otherwise the fruit is redeemed on less than a perutah.

options.label: C
options.text: Two separate coins.

options.label: D
options.text: The actual monetary value of the fruit.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish infers from Tosfos that the fruit needs to be redeemed on a perutah, while the chomesh needs a separate quarter perutah. If both redeemed on a single perutah, the fruit gets less than a perutah.

questions.text: A perutah chamurah is needed when your ma'aser sheini is worth less than a perutah. What makes a coin a perutah chamurah?

options.label: A
options.text: It is made of gold.

options.label: B
options.text: It was previously used to redeem ma'aser sheini worth at least a perutah.

options.label: C
options.text: It is worth at least 10 shekel.

options.label: D
options.text: It was minted in Eretz Yisrael.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: Chizkiyah teaches: if you already have a coin used to be podeh ma'aser sheini (ma'os rishonos), you may redeem less than a perutah on it. This coin is called a perutah chamurah.

questions.text: The Chazon Ish adds a further limitation to using a perutah chamurah. You may only use it to redeem ma'aser sheini worth less than a perutah if:

options.label: A
options.text: The coin was used on Shabbos.

options.label: B
options.text: The previous redemption on the coin was from produce of equal or higher halachic requirement than the current produce.

options.label: C
options.text: The coin is made of silver.

options.label: D
options.text: You add a chomesh.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish writes that you may only redeem ma'aser sheini worth less than a perutah if the coin was used for produce of equal or higher halachic requirement. You cannot redeem wine (biblical) using a coin that was used for cucumbers (rabbinic).

questions.text: Biur ma'aser (removing all owed tu"m from your house) must be performed on which years of the shemittah cycle?

options.label: A
options.text: Years 1 and 4.

options.label: B
options.text: Years 3 and 6.

options.label: C
options.text: Years 4 and 7, on erev the last day of Pesach.

options.label: D
options.text: Every year before Rosh Hashana.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam writes that on years 4 and 7 of the shemittah cycle, on erev the last day of Pesach, one is obligated to ensure all owed tu"m from the previous three years have been separated and given.

questions.text: Practically today, when performing biur ma'aser, the main thing that usually needs to be destroyed is:

options.label: A
options.text: The terumah gedolah produce.

options.label: B
options.text: The ma'aser rishon produce.

options.label: C
options.text: The ma'aser sheini coin.

options.label: D
options.text: The ma'aser ani produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: Practically today, in most cases the only thing that needs to be destroyed is the ma'aser sheini coin. If you are a member of an organization, they destroy it for you.

questions.text: The year of ma'aser for vegetables is determined by:

options.label: A
options.text: When the vegetable reached one-third of its growth.

options.label: B
options.text: When the vegetable was picked.

options.label: C
options.text: Tu B'Shvat.

options.label: D
options.text: When the vegetable was planted.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: Regarding vegetables, the determining factor is when it was picked. If picked before RH of year two, mafrish ma'aser sheini; if picked after RH, mafrish ma'aser ani.

questions.text: The year of ma'aser for grain and legumes (e.g., wheat, soy) is determined by:

options.label: A
options.text: When it was picked/harvested.

options.label: B
options.text: When it was planted.

options.label: C
options.text: Whether it reached one-third of its growth before or after Rosh Hashana.

options.label: D
options.text: Tu B'Shvat.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: Regarding grain and soy, the determining factor is whether they reached a third of their growth before or after Rosh Hashana (regardless of when they were picked).

questions.text: The year of ma'aser for fruit trees is calculated from:

options.label: A
options.text: Rosh Hashana, based on when picked.

options.label: B
options.text: Tu B'Shvat, based on whether it reached one-third of its growth before or after Tu B'Shvat.

options.label: C
options.text: Rosh Hashana, based on one-third growth.

options.label: D
options.text: Tu B'Shvat, based on when picked.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: Trees are similar to grain in that the determining factor is whether they grew a third, but differ because years are calculated from Tu B'Shvat, not Rosh Hashana.

questions.text: An esrog is unique because it combines aspects of both trees and vegetables. How is its year determined?

options.label: A
options.text: Like a tree: Tu B'Shvat, based on one-third growth.

options.label: B
options.text: Like a vegetable: Rosh Hashana, based on picking.

options.label: C
options.text: Tu B'Shvat (like a tree), but based on when it was picked (like a vegetable).

options.label: D
options.text: Rosh Hashana (like a vegetable), but based on one-third growth (like grain).

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam explains that an esrog resembles a tree (years from Tu B'Shvat) but resembles a vegetable (year determined by picking time, not one-third growth).

questions.text: There is a dispute about how to calculate the year of ma'aser for citrus fruits (oranges, lemons). The Chazon Ish passkens that:

options.label: A
options.text: They follow the regular fruit tree rules.

options.label: B
options.text: They resemble esrogim, so we are machmir and give them the stringencies of both trees and vegetables.

options.label: C
options.text: They are exempt from tu"m.

options.label: D
options.text: They follow the grain and soy rules.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish passkens that citrus fruits resemble esrogim. We are machmir and give them stringencies of both: if growing and picking span different years, separate both ma'aser sheini and ma'aser ani.

questions.text: RSZA disagrees with the Chazon Ish about citrus fruits. He passkens that:

options.label: A
options.text: Citrus fruits are calculated like other fruit trees – by one-third growth from Tu B'Shvat – and are NOT treated like esrogim.

options.label: B
options.text: Citrus are always ma'aser sheini.

options.label: C
options.text: Citrus are exempt from tu"m.

options.label: D
options.text: Citrus follow the vegetable rules.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: RSZA passkens that lemons and citrus are calculated like other fruit trees and are not considered an esrog. He proves this from the Rambam who says 'only' an esrog follows picking time.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): According to the Rambam, if you overestimate the amount of ma'aser rishon you need to give (e.g., give a full orange when only half was required), the remaining oranges are permitted, but the orange given as ma'aser rishon may not be eaten.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: TUM-F. Precisely Calculating the Amounts
sourceRef.snippet: The S"A passkens that if one overestimates the ma'aser, the remaining fruits are permitted. However, the given orange may not be eaten since only half is ma'aser rishon and the other half remains tevel.

questions.text: If you carefully recite the standard nussach of hafrashas tu"m, you will separate the exact amounts required, as long as you:

options.label: A
options.text: Counted every single fruit.

options.label: B
options.text: Made sure you separated at least a bit more than 1% of the produce.

options.label: C
options.text: Used a certified scale.

options.label: D
options.text: Had a witness present.

sourceRef.section: TUM-F. Precisely Calculating the Amounts
sourceRef.snippet: The Chukei De'ah points out that today, if you carefully recite the nussach and separate more than 1%, you are actually separating the exact amounts required.

questions.text: The Chazon Ish writes that if someone is away from home and doesn't have the full nussach, he should:

options.label: A
options.text: Wait until he gets home.

options.label: B
options.text: Separate more than 1% and say 'the hafrashas tu"m should be done as specified in the accepted nussach.'

options.label: C
options.text: Not eat the produce.

options.label: D
options.text: Find a kohen to do it for him.

sourceRef.section: TUM-G. Shortened Nussach & Allocating Location
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish writes that one should separate more than 1% and say that the hafrashas tu"m should be done as specified in the accepted nussach.

questions.text: Why should the shortened nussach be used when being mafrish tu"m from a liquid in motion (e.g., wine being poured)?

options.label: A
options.text: Because liquids are exempt from the full nussach.

options.label: B
options.text: Because the liquid may shift between designations – the 'north' for ma'aser rishon may move to the 'south' and conflict with ma'aser sheini/ani.

options.label: C
options.text: Because the bracha is different for liquids.

options.label: D
options.text: Because liquid tu"m is only rabbinic.

sourceRef.section: TUM-G. Shortened Nussach & Allocating Location
sourceRef.snippet: The Derech Emunah explains that with moving liquid, after you allocate the 'north' for ma'aser rishon, it may shift to the 'south' and you can no longer allocate the south for ma'aser sheini. The shortened nussach makes all tu"m take effect simultaneously.

questions.text: The Torah only lists three types of produce as being required in tu"m. According to the Chazon Ish and the majority of poskim, only these are biblically obligated. What are they?

options.label: A
options.text: Wheat, barley, and spelt.

options.label: B
options.text: Grain, wine, and oil.

options.label: C
options.text: All fruits and vegetables.

options.label: D
options.text: The five grains.

sourceRef.section: TUM-H. Obligation Today
sourceRef.snippet: The Torah lists 'reshis degancha, tiroshcha v'yitzharecha' – grain, wine and oil. The Chazon Ish passkens like the majority that only these are biblically obligated. Other fruits are rabbinically obligated; vegetables are also rabbinic.

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): Only produce grown in Eretz Yisrael is obligated in hafrashas tu"m.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: TUM-H. Obligation Today
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam (Terumos 1:1) passkens that only produce grown in Eretz Yisrael is obligated in tu"m. The Sha"ch explains that any mitzvah dependent on the land only applies in Eretz Yisrael.

questions.text: What is the status of Eilat regarding hafrashas tu"m?

options.label: A
options.text: All agree it is part of Eretz Yisrael and requires hafrasha with a bracha.

options.label: B
options.text: The Chazon Ish passkens it is chutz la'aretz; others say to be mafrish without a bracha; the Ir HaKodesh says with a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: Eilat is completely exempt from tu"m.

options.label: D
options.text: Eilat follows the same halacha as Gaza.

sourceRef.section: TUM-H. Obligation Today
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish passkens Eilat is chutz la'aretz. The Mo'adim U'Zmanim quotes Rav Epstein: mafrish without a bracha. The Ir HaKodesh passkens Eilat is part of EY – mafrish with a bracha.

questions.text: Regarding the dispute between the Rambam and the Ra'avad about the status of terumah today: the S"A passkens that:

options.label: A
options.text: Terumah is biblical today even without a majority in EY (like the Ra'avad).

options.label: B
options.text: Terumah is only rabbinic today since the majority of Jews are not in EY (like the Rambam).

options.label: C
options.text: Terumah does not apply at all today.

options.label: D
options.text: Terumah is biblical for grain but rabbinic for other produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-H. Obligation Today
sourceRef.snippet: The S"A (siman 331:2) passkens like the Rambam that terumah is only rabbinic today. The Rama quotes the Ra'avad but agrees with the S"A's pssak.

questions.text: According to the Chazon Ish, tea leaves placed in hot water for flavor and then removed are:

options.label: A
options.text: Obligated in tu"m with a bracha.

options.label: B
options.text: Obligated in tu"m without a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: Exempt from tu"m – they are like black pepper which is added only for flavor and not consumed.

options.label: D
options.text: Subject to a dispute and one should be machmir.

sourceRef.section: TUM-I. Spices
sourceRef.snippet: Tea leaves added just for flavor and then removed are similar to black pepper and not obligated in tu"m. However, coffee, where the intention is to consume the beans, is obligated.
