questions.text: The Beis Midrash L'Halacha B'Hisyashvus differentiates between three categories of spices for tu"m. Which of the following pairings are correct? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Spices for flavor only (e.g., tea, black pepper, bay leaves) – exempt from tu"m.

options.label: B
options.text: Foods consumed as food but also used as spices (e.g., onions, garlic, parsley) – mafrish with a bracha.

options.label: C
options.text: Spices added for flavor but also intended to be consumed (e.g., coffee, ginger, oregano) – mafrish without a bracha.

options.label: D
options.text: All spices are exempt from tu"m.

sourceRef.section: TUM-I. Spices
sourceRef.snippet: Three categories: 1) Flavor only (tea, pepper, bay leaves) = exempt. 2) Consumed as food and as spice (onions, garlic, parsley) = mafrish with bracha. 3) For spice but also consumed (coffee, ginger, oregano) = mafrish without bracha.

questions.text: A vegetable grown in a pot with a hole (perforated pot) on an open balcony is:

options.label: A
options.text: Completely exempt from tu"m.

options.label: B
options.text: Biblically obligated in tu"m.

options.label: C
options.text: Only rabbinically obligated.

options.label: D
options.text: Obligated only if the pot is on the ground floor.

sourceRef.section: TUM-J. Potted Plants
sourceRef.snippet: Tosfos in Gittin (7b) writes that even if the pot is suspended in the air, if it has a hole, it is biblically required in tu"m as long as nothing is between the pot and the floor.

questions.text: What is the status of produce grown indoors (not in a greenhouse)?

options.label: A
options.text: Biblically obligated in tu"m.

options.label: B
options.text: Best to be mafrish without a bracha – the Rambam says rabbinic, but the Ra'avad exempts completely.

options.label: C
options.text: Completely exempt according to all opinions.

options.label: D
options.text: Obligated with a bracha like outdoor produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-J. Potted Plants
sourceRef.snippet: The Rambam exempts produce grown inside biblically (since the passuk says 'hayotzei hasadeh' – out in the field), but Chazal still required hafrasha. The Ra'avad exempts completely. Poskim say mafrish without a bracha.

questions.text: Rav Chaim Kanievsky passkens that plants grown in greenhouses are:

options.label: A
options.text: Exempt from tu"m since they are indoors.

options.label: B
options.text: Only rabbinically obligated.

options.label: C
options.text: Biblically obligated – since the greenhouse is the normal and best way to grow plants, it is considered 'the field.'

options.label: D
options.text: Subject to a safek and should be mafrish without a bracha.

sourceRef.section: TUM-J. Potted Plants
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Chaim Kanievsky (Derech Emunah) passkens that greenhouses are biblically obligated in hafrashas tu"m since the cover helps the produce grow and it is the normal way to grow, so it's considered 'the field.'

questions.text: True (A) or False (B): Mushrooms are exempt from hafrashas tu"m because they do not absorb their nutrients from the ground.

options.label: A
options.text: True

options.label: B
options.text: False

sourceRef.section: TUM-J. Potted Plants
sourceRef.snippet: The Shevet HaLevi passkens that mushrooms are exempt from hafrashas tu"m since they grow from the air and do not absorb from the ground, unlike grain, oil and wine.

questions.text: Which of the following are among the six criteria of kevi'us l'ma'aser (acts that indicate produce is ready for formal consumption and trigger the obligation to be mafrish)? (Click as many as apply)

options.label: A
options.text: The produce enters your courtyard.

options.label: B
options.text: You sell part of the produce.

options.label: C
options.text: You cook or salt the produce.

options.label: D
options.text: You recite a bracha on the produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-K. Stage of Requirement
sourceRef.snippet: The six criteria of kevi'us l'ma'aser are: 1) Produce entering courtyard. 2) Selling part. 3) Cooking. 4) Salting. 5) Being mafrish even just terumah. 6) When Shabbos arrives.

questions.text: The obligation to be mafrish and the prohibition to eat even b'derech arai go into effect when two criteria are both met. What are they?

options.label: A
options.text: The fruit must be ripe AND it must be Shabbos.

options.label: B
options.text: Gemar melacha (processing complete) AND one of the six criteria of kevi'us l'ma'aser.

options.label: C
options.text: The fruit must be picked AND brought to Yerushalayim.

options.label: D
options.text: The fruit must be sold AND a bracha must be recited.

sourceRef.section: TUM-K. Stage of Requirement
sourceRef.snippet: The obligation to be mafrish and the prohibition to eat even b'derech arai go into effect when two criteria are met: 1) gemar melacha – processing is complete, and 2) kevi'us l'ma'aser – one of the 6 criteria.

questions.text: The Chazon Ish explains that whether eating is considered arai (non-permanent) or keva (permanent) is determined by:

options.label: A
options.text: The amount of food eaten.

options.label: B
options.text: Your intention – if you designated the food for a meal, it is keva; if eating on the go, even a lot, it is arai.

options.label: C
options.text: Whether you are sitting or standing.

options.label: D
options.text: Whether you washed your hands before eating.

sourceRef.section: TUM-K. Stage of Requirement
sourceRef.snippet: The Chazon Ish explains that arai and keva are not determined by the amount eaten but by intention. Designated for a meal = keva. Eating on the go, even a lot = arai.

questions.text: In your garden, you may eat fruit without being mafrish ONLY if:

options.label: A
options.text: You eat fewer than 3 fruits.

options.label: B
options.text: You take one fruit at a time and only if the entire fruit can fit in your mouth in one shot (e.g., a grape or cherry).

options.label: C
options.text: You are standing.

options.label: D
options.text: The fruit tree is less than 3 years old.

sourceRef.section: TUM-K. Stage of Requirement
sourceRef.snippet: The kashrus HaMazon passkens that any fruit that can be eaten in one shot (grape, cherry, nut) may be eaten in the garden, but fruits that cannot (pomegranate, orange) may not be taken off the tree and eaten.

questions.text: At a "pick your own farm" in Israel, the simplest way to eat freely while picking (according to all opinions including the Chazon Ish) is:

options.label: A
options.text: Be mafrish before picking.

options.label: B
options.text: Pick only one small fruit at a time and eat it.

options.label: C
options.text: Pay at the end so there is no kevi'us l'ma'aser from purchasing.

options.label: D
options.text: Both B and C work according to all opinions.

sourceRef.section: TUM-L. Pick Your Own Farm Produce
sourceRef.snippet: Two solutions work even according to the Chazon Ish: 1) Only pick one fruit at a time if it's small. 2) Pay at the end so there is no kevi'us l'ma'aser.

questions.text: The Rambam differentiates between purchasing a tree for its fruit and purchasing a specific number of fruits. When you buy specific fruits and pay upfront:

options.label: A
options.text: You may eat freely because you own the fruit.

options.label: B
options.text: The moment you pick the fruits, the sale goes into effect (kevi'us l'ma'aser) and picking more than one is gemar melacha – so you must be mafrish.

options.label: C
options.text: You are exempt since the field owner already separated tu"m.

options.label: D
options.text: You may eat b'derech arai without limit.

sourceRef.section: TUM-L. Pick Your Own Farm Produce
sourceRef.snippet: Rav Achitov explains: when you buy specific fruits and pay upfront, the moment you pick them is when the sale goes into effect (kevi'us l'ma'aser), and planning to eat them now is gemar melacha.

questions.text: The S"A passkens that hefker fruit is:

options.label: A
options.text: Obligated in tu"m just like owned produce.

options.label: B
options.text: Exempt from tu"m.

options.label: C
options.text: Only obligated if collected in large amounts.

options.label: D
options.text: Exempt only in chutz la'aretz.

sourceRef.section: TUM-M. Miscellaneous Halachos
sourceRef.snippet: The S"A (siman 331:16) passkens that hefker fruit is exempt from tu"m. If you live in a city with fruit trees and the municipality allows you to take the fruit, you do not need to be mafrish.

questions.text: Fruits grown in Eretz Yisrael and shipped to chutz la'aretz are:

options.label: A
options.text: Exempt from tu"m once they leave Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: B
options.text: Still obligated in hafrashas tu"m.

options.label: C
options.text: Only obligated if eaten by someone living in Eretz Yisrael.

options.label: D
options.text: Only obligated for grain, wine, and oil.

sourceRef.section: TUM-M. Miscellaneous Halachos
sourceRef.snippet: Fruits grown in EY and shipped to chutz la'aretz are obligated in tu"m. This is common with Israeli oranges sent to England and cherry tomatoes shipped to the US.

questions.text: The Shemiras Shabbos Kehilchasa passkens that if you are unable to separate tu"m before Shabbos, you may:

options.label: A
options.text: Be mafrish on Shabbos without any preparation.

options.label: B
options.text: Not eat the produce until after Shabbos.

options.label: C
options.text: Say the nussach of the hafrasha on erev Shabbos in the future tense, then on Shabbos recite a bracha and say the regular nussach.

options.label: D
options.text: Ask a non-Jew to be mafrish for you.

sourceRef.section: TUM-M. Miscellaneous Halachos
sourceRef.snippet: The SSK passkens that you may say the nussach on erev Shabbos in the future tense, then on Shabbos recite a bracha and say the regular nussach. Since it's a derabanan, Chazal were lenient when the nussach begins on erev Shabbos.

questions.text: Describe the four types of terumos and ma'asros. For each one, explain: who it is given to, the amount required, which years it is given, and any notable halachos.
questions.modelAnswer: 1) Terumah Gedolah: Given to the kohen. Biblically, any amount suffices; Chazal set gradations: generous = 1/40, average = 1/50, stingy = 1/60. Today we separate the smallest amount since it's not actually given to a kohen. Given every year except shemittah. Notable: Only tahor kohanim may eat it. The Yisrael chooses which kohen receives it. 2) Ma'aser Rishon: Given to the levi. 10% of remaining produce after terumah gedolah. Given every year except shemittah. Notable: Has no kedusha and can be eaten by anyone. Ezra fined levi'im and gave it to kohanim when they didn't return. Today, Chazon Ish holds we don't give to levi'im since we can't verify lineage; Teshuvos V'Hanhagos argues we should. 3) Terumas Ma'aser: The levi gives 10% of his ma'aser rishon (=1% of original produce) to a kohen. Given every year except shemittah. Notable: Has kedusha like terumah – only eaten by tahor kohanim. The Yisrael may separate it himself if he wants. 4) Ma'aser Sheini/Ani: On years 1,2,4,5 – Ma'aser Sheini: 10% of remaining produce set aside and eaten in Yerushalayim. Can be redeemed onto a coin (add chomesh if redeeming your own). On years 3,6 – Ma'aser Ani: 10% given to poor people. Has no kedusha. Must actually be given to an ani; if not separated, food remains tevel.

sourceRef.section: TUM-B. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros
sourceRef.snippet: Four gifts: Terumah Gedolah (any amount → kohen), Ma'aser Rishon (10% → levi), Terumas Ma'aser (1% → kohen), Ma'aser Sheini (years 1,2,4,5 → eaten in Yerushalayim) or Ma'aser Ani (years 3,6 → poor).

questions.text: Explain the concept of perutah chamurah. Why is it needed, what makes a coin a perutah chamurah, what is the dispute between Rav Kook and RSZA, and what practical limitation does the Chazon Ish add?
questions.modelAnswer: A perutah chamurah is needed when your ma'aser sheini produce is worth less than a perutah – in which case it cannot be redeemed on an ordinary coin. The Gemara (Bava Metzia 53a) teaches that if you already have a coin that was previously used to redeem ma'aser sheini worth at least a perutah ('ma'os rishonos'), you may use the excess value on that coin to redeem ma'aser sheini worth less than a perutah. Dispute: Rav Kook holds that today, since we redeem large quantities on exactly one perutah, there is no excess. Therefore, the first time you use a coin, you must redeem the actual monetary value of the ma'aser sheini (not just a perutah) to create real excess. RSZA disagrees – he argues that even today there is minor excess from rounding up the perutah value and exchange rate, so any coin previously used for redemption works. Chazon Ish's limitation: The perutah chamurah may only be used to redeem produce of equal or lesser halachic requirement than the produce originally redeemed on it. E.g., a coin used for cucumbers (rabbinic obligation) cannot redeem wine (biblical obligation) worth less than a perutah. A 'super perutah chamurah' – created by redeeming grain, wine or oil grown in the center of EY – may be used for any produce.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: Perutah chamurah = coin previously used for ma'aser sheini worth ≥ perutah. Rav Kook: must redeem actual value first. RSZA: rounding creates excess. Chazon Ish: must match halachic level of obligation.

questions.text: Compare and contrast the Torah Ve Ha'aretz Institute and the Beis Midrash L'Halacha B'Hisyashvus regarding hafrashas tu"m. Discuss at least five differences between the two organizations.
questions.modelAnswer: 1) Perutah for redemption: Torah Ve Ha'aretz redeems on a perutah plus a chomesh (quarter perutah); Beis Midrash redeems on a single perutah. 2) Coin ownership: Torah Ve Ha'aretz gives each member their own coin; Beis Midrash uses Rav Reichenberg's coin – members use his coin, so it's as if someone else is redeeming for you. 3) Chomesh on fruit: Torah Ve Ha'aretz members own their coin, so they must add chomesh to the fruit value; Beis Midrash members don't add chomesh since it's as if someone else redeems. However, this is dangerous – if you accidentally use the regular nussach that includes 'v'chumsho,' the redemption is invalid according to RSZA. 4) Perutah chamurah creation: Torah Ve Ha'aretz is choshesh for Rav Kook and transfers actual value of produce to the coin; Beis Midrash transfers one perutah. 5) Availability after biur: Torah Ve Ha'aretz gets a perutah chamurah around Shavuos (when wheat is harvested); Beis Midrash has one ready on isru chag by crushing grapes for wine on year 2 and sifting out pits/peels after Pesach. 6) Ma'aser ani: Torah Ve Ha'aretz uses the concept of mekiri kehunah – a designated gabbay tzedakah automatically acquires the ma'aser ani; Beis Midrash requires someone to lift the produce on behalf of the ani. 7) Ma'aser rishon: Torah Ve Ha'aretz has a designated levi through mekiri (included in membership); Beis Midrash requires finding a levi yourself or recording the debt.

sourceRef.section: TUM-D. Hafrashas Terumos and Ma'asros Today — The Hafrasha
sourceRef.snippet: Seven differences: perutah amount, coin ownership, chomesh requirement, perutah chamurah method, availability post-biur, ma'aser ani process, ma'aser rishon process.

questions.text: Explain the halachic framework for when produce becomes obligated in hafrashas tu"m. Include: gemar melacha, kevi'us l'ma'aser, the difference between eating in a field vs. garden vs. home, and practical applications for eating fruit in your garden.
questions.modelAnswer: The obligation to be mafrish and the prohibition to eat even b'derech arai go into effect when two conditions are met: 1) Gemar melacha – the produce completed processing and is ready to be sold. Each produce has its own standard – today, placing produce in boxes for sale is generally considered gemar melacha. 2) Kevi'us l'ma'aser – one of six criteria: produce enters your courtyard, selling part, cooking, salting, being mafrish terumah, or Shabbos arrives. In the field: Before gemar melacha, you may eat freely b'derech arai. Even after gemar melacha, if planning to bring home (not sell), you may eat arai until a kevi'us criterion is met. In the garden: The garden is your courtyard (kevi'us), and picking more than one fruit is gemar melacha – both conditions are met simultaneously. Solution: the Rambam teaches that taking one fruit at a time is not gemar melacha. The Chazon Ish and poskim passkin you may eat one fruit at a time if the entire fruit fits in your mouth in one shot (grape, cherry, nut). But oranges or pomegranates cannot be eaten this way; you'd need to peel them while still attached. At home: Once produce enters the home with gemar melacha, you may not eat even arai. If there was no gemar melacha (e.g., zucchini brought home with yellow membrane), you may still eat arai.

sourceRef.section: TUM-K. Stage of Requirement
sourceRef.snippet: Two criteria trigger obligation: gemar melacha + kevi'us l'ma'aser. Field = arai OK before gemar melacha. Garden = one fruit at a time only. Home = forbidden once both criteria met.

questions.text: Explain how to calculate the year of ma'aser for different types of produce: vegetables, grain/legumes, fruit trees, esrog, and citrus. Include the dispute between the Chazon Ish and RSZA regarding citrus fruits.
questions.modelAnswer: 1) Vegetables: Year counted from Rosh Hashana. Determining factor = when picked (lekitah). If picked before RH of year 3 → year 2 (ma'aser sheini). If picked after RH → year 3 (ma'aser ani). 2) Grain/Legumes (wheat, soy): Year counted from Rosh Hashana. Determining factor = when reached 1/3 growth (hava'as shlish). Picking time irrelevant. If grew 1/3 before RH of year 3 → year 2. 3) Fruit Trees: Year counted from Tu B'Shvat. Determining factor = when reached 1/3 growth (chanatah). Picking time irrelevant. A pomegranate that grew 1/3 after Tu B'Shvat of year 2, even if picked after RH of year 3, takes ma'aser sheini. 4) Esrog: Combination – year counted from Tu B'Shvat (like tree) but determining factor = when picked (like vegetable). 5) Citrus (oranges, lemons): The Chazon Ish passkens they resemble esrogim in many ways and we are machmir – give them stringencies of both trees and vegetables. If growing and picking span different years (common with citrus), separate both ma'aser sheini AND ma'aser ani. RSZA disagrees: citrus are calculated like other fruit trees (by 1/3 growth from Tu B'Shvat). He proves from the Rambam who says 'only' an esrog follows picking; since lemons were known in the Rambam's time, the word 'only' excludes them.

sourceRef.section: TUM-E. Calculating the Years of Ma'aser
sourceRef.snippet: Vegetables: RH + picking. Grain: RH + 1/3 growth. Trees: Tu B'Shvat + 1/3 growth. Esrog: Tu B'Shvat + picking. Citrus: Chazon Ish = stringencies of both; RSZA = like regular trees.
